title: Digital Foundations, Computers, and AI in Everyday Life

learning_objectives: Understand what a computer is and how it helps users complete tasks.
Distinguish hardware, software, operating systems, files, folders, and apps.
Explain AI in plain language without confusing it with general software.
Recognize common places where AI already appears in everyday life.
Build confidence using simple technical vocabulary.
expected_outcomes: Students can explain core digital concepts using beginner-friendly language.
Students can describe the difference between a normal software program and an AI-enabled system.
Students can identify at least five daily-life examples of AI use.

lesson.title: What Is a Computer and Where Is AI Around Us?

blocks.title: Week 1 Start
blocks.content: This week builds digital confidence first. Students learn how devices, apps, files, and AI-enabled experiences fit together.

blocks.title: Learning Objectives
items: Understand what a computer does using input, process, and output.
Separate hardware from software using practical examples.
Recognize that not every smart-looking app is AI.
Use plain language to explain AI in daily life.

items.label: Computer
items.description: An electronic machine that receives input, processes instructions, stores information, and produces output.

items.label: Hardware
items.description: Physical parts such as screen, keyboard, or microphone.

items.label: Software
items.description: Instructions and apps that tell hardware what to do.

items.label: AI
items.description: Systems that handle tasks involving pattern recognition, language, or judgment-like behavior.

items.ai: Uses learned patterns or probabilistic output
Can vary by context and phrasing

blocks.title: Responsible AI Reminder
blocks.content: An AI response can be useful and still be wrong. Students should learn verification from the first week.

activity.title: AI or Not AI?
activity.instructions: Sort each example into AI or Not AI based on whether it uses learned patterns or human-like decision support.
activity.success_criteria: Correctly classify at least 4 of 5 examples.

questions.prompt: Which option is the best example of hardware?
questions.explanation: Hardware refers to the physical parts of a device.

questions.prompt: Which example is most likely using AI?
questions.explanation: Spam filters commonly rely on pattern recognition and classification.

options: How a device receives, works on, and returns information
How an internet bill is paid
questions.explanation: Input-process-output is a basic model for understanding how computing systems handle tasks.

reflection.prompt: Write 3 to 5 lines about where you already see AI in your daily life and one place where you should be careful before trusting the result.
reflection.placeholder: Example: I see AI in maps, recommendations, and spam filtering. I should still verify important information because AI can sound confident and still be wrong.

teacher_notes: Start by opening an app, typing a note, saving it, closing it, and reopening it.
Use side-by-side examples to separate hardware, software, and AI behavior.
Keep reminding students that AI can be impressive and still make mistakes.
